stem:
A 58-year-old man with type 2 diabetes attends your general practice for cardiac rehab follow up six weeks after an inferior STEMI managed with primary PCI to the right coronary artery. He has been smoking 25 cigarettes a day for 40 years and was unable to quit on his own after his admission, although he has cut down to 12 a day. He has no history of depression, psychosis, suicidality or seizures. He is currently on aspirin 100 mg, ticagrelor 90 mg twice daily, atorvastatin 80 mg, metoprolol 50 mg twice daily, perindopril 5 mg, metformin 1 g twice daily and empagliflozin 10 mg.

His cardiac rehab clinician sent him to discuss pharmacotherapy options. He is highly motivated to quit before his upcoming holiday in three months, has tried nicotine patches alone twice in the past without success, and his wife is supportive. His BP today is 128/78 mmHg and pulse 72/min. He has no liver disease, no psychiatric history, and his eGFR is above 60 mL/min. He asks which medication is most likely to help him quit.

data_table.Vital signs: Pulse 72/min, BP 128/78 mmHg, RR 14/min, SpO2 98% room air, temperature 36.7 °C
data_table.Examination: No respiratory or cardiovascular signs; weight 92 kg; BMI 30
data_table.Investigations: eGFR >60 mL/min; LFTs within normal limits; HbA1c 6.6%; lipids on target

lead_in: What is the most appropriate pharmacotherapy?

options.text: Combination nicotine replacement: 21 mg patch daily plus 4 mg lozenge as needed for 12 weeks
options.rationale: Combination NRT outperforms single NRT, but varenicline retains the highest single-agent efficacy; combination NRT is the preferred next-line when varenicline is contraindicated or not tolerated. (Source: RACGP Smoking Cessation Guidelines)

options.text: Bupropion 150 mg orally daily for 3 days then 150 mg twice daily for 9 weeks total
options.rationale: Bupropion is effective but has roughly half the absolute quit rate of varenicline; it is also contraindicated in patients with seizure risk and lowers seizure threshold. (Source: RACGP Smoking Cessation Guidelines)

options.text: Varenicline with the standard week 1 dose escalation then 1 mg twice daily for 11 weeks
options.rationale: Varenicline is the most effective single agent for smoking cessation (NNT approximately 8 versus placebo) and the EAGLES trial confirmed no excess cardiovascular or neuropsychiatric events; it is preferred over single-agent NRT, particularly after failed NRT alone. (Source: RACGP Smoking Cessation Guidelines; EAGLES trial; AMH)

options.text: Nortriptyline 75 mg orally nocte titrated upwards as a third line off-label cessation aid
options.rationale: Nortriptyline has demonstrated efficacy in smoking cessation but is third-line in Australia given anticholinergic burden and TCA cardiotoxicity, particularly post-MI. (Source: RACGP Smoking Cessation Guidelines)

options.text: E-cigarettes nicotine vaping liquid at a community pharmacy under the TGA prescription pathway
options.rationale: Nicotine vaping is available in Australia by prescription as a second-line cessation aid when first-line pharmacotherapies have failed; first-line varenicline should be offered first. (Source: TGA Nicotine Vaping Products; RACGP Smoking Cessation Guidelines)

explanation.summary: Smoking cessation in Australia follows a stepped approach: brief intervention with the 5As (Ask, Advise, Assess, Assist, Arrange), behavioural support and pharmacotherapy. First-line agents are varenicline (12 weeks, dose escalation in week 1), combination nicotine replacement (long-acting patch plus short-acting form), and bupropion. Varenicline has the highest absolute quit rate. The EAGLES trial demonstrated no excess cardiovascular or neuropsychiatric events with varenicline, dispelling earlier concerns and supporting its use in patients with stable cardiovascular and mental health comorbidity. Nicotine vaping is restricted to prescription-only and is positioned as second-line by the TGA. Cardiac rehab is the ideal cessation window post-MI.
key_points: Varenicline = highest absolute quit rate (NNT ~8)
EAGLES trial: no excess CV or neuropsychiatric events
Combination NRT outperforms single NRT
Nicotine vaping is prescription-only, second-line in Australia
explanation.pearls: Varenicline post-ACS is safe per EAGLES and AHA secondary-prevention statements; the dose escalation (0.5 mg daily x 3 days, 0.5 mg twice daily x 4 days, then 1 mg twice daily for 11 weeks) is the standard 12-week course.

stem:
You are the GP registrar at a community refugee health clinic seeing a 34-year-old woman who arrived in Australia six months ago on a humanitarian visa from a country of recent armed conflict. She lives with her three children in resettlement housing. She presents with sleep disturbance, intrusive flashbacks of the conflict, hypervigilance and avoidance of news media for the last 4 months. She speaks limited English and is accompanied by a female accredited interpreter she has worked with before.

She denies current suicidal ideation. She drinks no alcohol and does not use substances. She has no past psychiatric history. She is medically well; her physical examination is unremarkable. Her PCL-5 (administered with the interpreter using a validated translation) scores 52. She is reluctant to take medication and asks what other options exist. The clinic has access to a trauma-trained psychologist who works with an interpreter and a community-based peer support program for women from her language group.

data_table.Vital signs: Pulse 78/min, BP 118/72 mmHg, RR 14/min, SpO2 99% room air, temperature 36.7 °C
data_table.Mental state: Hypervigilant; affect anxious; thought form linear; no psychotic features; insight preserved; PCL-5 score 52
data_table.History: Refugee from recent conflict; 6 months in Australia; safe housing; no current suicidality; no substance use

lead_in: What is the most appropriate first-line intervention?

options.text: Refer to community peer support program alone without psychological therapy
options.rationale: Peer support is a valuable adjunct in refugee settings but is not a substitute for evidence-based trauma-focused therapy in established PTSD. (Source: RACGP Refugee Health)

options.text: Commence sertraline 50 mg daily and titrate up over 4 weeks before therapy referral
options.rationale: SSRIs are second-line for PTSD when trauma-focused therapy is not available or declined; her preference and clinic access favour therapy first. (Source: Phoenix Australia ASD/PTSD Guidelines)

options.text: Prescribe quetiapine 50 mg nocte for sleep and hypervigilance before specialist review
options.rationale: Atypical antipsychotics are not first-line for PTSD and carry metabolic risk; sleep hygiene plus trauma-focused therapy is preferred. (Source: Phoenix Australia ASD/PTSD Guidelines)

options.text: Recommend supportive counselling and watchful waiting given recent resettlement
options.rationale: Six months of established PTSD symptoms in a refugee with high PCL-5 score warrants active trauma-focused treatment; passive monitoring delays recovery. (Source: Phoenix Australia ASD/PTSD Guidelines)

options.text: Refer for trauma-focused cognitive behavioural therapy with the interpreter-supported psychologist
options.rationale: Trauma-focused psychological therapy (TF-CBT, EMDR, or cognitive processing therapy) is the first-line treatment for PTSD across all guidelines, and is preferred over medication unless not available; cultural and language-appropriate delivery is essential. (Source: Phoenix Australia ASD/PTSD Guidelines; RACGP Refugee Health)

explanation.summary: Post-traumatic stress disorder in refugee and asylum-seeker populations has a prevalence of 30-50% depending on conflict exposure and post-migration stressors. First-line treatment is trauma-focused psychological therapy: TF-CBT, EMDR, or cognitive processing therapy. Therapy should be delivered in a culturally appropriate way, often with an accredited interpreter familiar with mental health vocabulary. Pharmacotherapy (SSRIs such as sertraline or paroxetine) is second-line and useful when therapy is unavailable, declined or when comorbid major depression is severe. Benzodiazepines are NOT recommended (worsen PTSD outcomes). Post-migration stressors (housing, finances, family reunion, immigration status) must be addressed as social determinants alongside symptom-focused care.
key_points: First line PTSD: trauma-focused CBT, EMDR, or cognitive processing therapy
SSRIs (sertraline, paroxetine) are second-line
Benzodiazepines avoided
Address post-migration stressors as part of care
explanation.pearls: Phoenix Australia guidelines place trauma-focused psychological therapy (TF-CBT, EMDR, cognitive processing therapy) as first-line for PTSD across populations; benzodiazepines are explicitly discouraged because they worsen long-term outcomes.

stem:
You are the ED consultant called to assess a 47-year-old man with treatment-resistant depression brought in by his wife with confusion and agitation that began over the last 12 hours. His regular medications are escitalopram 20 mg, mirtazapine 30 mg and olanzapine 10 mg nocte, all stable for 6 months. Three days ago his GP added tramadol 50 mg three times daily for back pain. He has been increasingly restless, with shaking of all four limbs.

On examination he is agitated and diaphoretic. Pulse is 132/min, BP 168/96 mmHg, respiratory rate 24/min and temperature 39.3 °C. Lower limb reflexes are markedly hyperactive with sustained ankle clonus bilaterally; upper limb reflexes are also brisk. There is intermittent myoclonus of the lower limbs. Pupils are 6 mm and reactive. Tone is increased in the lower limbs more than the upper limbs but there is no lead-pipe rigidity. Bowel sounds are hyperactive. CK is 980 U/L. He has been on this antipsychotic regimen without change.

data_table.Vital signs: Pulse 132/min, BP 168/96 mmHg, RR 24/min, SpO2 96% room air, temperature 39.3 °C
data_table.Examination: Agitated, diaphoretic; lower limb hyperreflexia with sustained ankle clonus bilaterally; myoclonus lower limbs; tone increased LL > UL, no lead-pipe rigidity; hyperactive bowel sounds; pupils 6 mm reactive
data_table.Investigations: CK 980 U/L (<200); WCC 11.4 x10^9/L; sodium, urea, creatinine within normal limits

lead_in: What is the most appropriate next step?

options.text: Start IV ceftriaxone and perform lumbar puncture for presumed acute meningoencephalitis pathway
options.rationale: Without meningism, focal neurology or CSF features and with classical serotonin toxicity from a new tramadol prescription, broad spectrum antibiotics and LP are not the priority. (Source: Therapeutic Guidelines Antibiotic)

options.text: Cease olanzapine and start IV dantrolene 2.5 mg/kg for neuroleptic malignant syndrome
options.rationale: NMS would be expected to show lead-pipe rigidity (axial dominant) with bradyreflexia and develops over days to weeks with antipsychotic exposure or dose change; absent these features and given new tramadol introduction the diagnosis is serotonin syndrome, not NMS. (Source: RANZCP CPG NMS vs Serotonin Syndrome)
source_refs: RANZCP CPG NMS vs Serotonin Syndrome

options.text: Administer paracetamol 1 g for fever and arrange psychiatric admission for medication review
options.rationale: Paracetamol does little for centrally driven hyperthermia in serotonin syndrome (active cooling and benzodiazepines work) and admission without serotonergic cessation does not address the cause. (Source: Therapeutic Guidelines Toxicology)

options.text: Give oral bromocriptine 2.5 mg and stop olanzapine immediately for presumed neuroleptic syndrome
options.rationale: Bromocriptine is an NMS adjunct, not a serotonin syndrome treatment; pushing dopaminergic activity does not address the syndrome and delays the correct withdrawal of serotonergic agents. (Source: RANZCP CPG NMS vs Serotonin Syndrome)
source_refs: RANZCP CPG NMS vs Serotonin Syndrome

options.text: Cease serotonergic agents, give IV crystalloid, start IV midazolam, admit to a cardiac-monitored bed
options.rationale: The Hunter Toxicity Criteria are met (clonus plus agitation plus diaphoresis plus hyperthermia in a serotonergic context) and the addition of tramadol to escitalopram and mirtazapine is the precipitant; serotonin syndrome management is rapid serotonergic withdrawal, supportive care including aggressive cooling and benzodiazepines, with cyproheptadine reserved for severe or refractory cases. (Source: Therapeutic Guidelines Toxicology; Hunter Serotonin Toxicity Criteria)

explanation.summary: Serotonin syndrome and neuroleptic malignant syndrome both present with autonomic instability, altered mental status and elevated temperature, but their neuromuscular phenotypes differ critically. Serotonin syndrome is dominated by hyperreflexia, clonus (especially lower limb), myoclonus, agitation and dilated pupils, develops within hours of a serotonergic addition or dose change, and resolves rapidly when offending agents are stopped. NMS is dominated by lead-pipe (axial) rigidity, bradyreflexia, severe hyperthermia, marked CK elevation (often >1000 U/L) and autonomic instability, develops over days to weeks of antipsychotic exposure or dose change, and resolves more slowly. The Hunter Criteria for serotonin toxicity require ingestion of a serotonergic agent plus one of: spontaneous clonus, inducible clonus plus agitation or diaphoresis, ocular clonus plus agitation or diaphoresis, tremor plus hyperreflexia, or hypertonia plus T >38 °C plus ocular or inducible clonus.
key_points: Serotonin syndrome: clonus, hyperreflexia, mydriasis, agitation; hours after serotonergic change
NMS: lead-pipe rigidity, bradyreflexia, marked CK rise, days-weeks after antipsychotic change
Tramadol plus SSRI is a classic serotonin syndrome trigger
Cyproheptadine reserved for severe/refractory serotonin syndrome
explanation.pearls: Lower-limb-dominant hyperreflexia with sustained ankle clonus plus agitation plus T >38 °C after a serotonergic addition (especially tramadol or linezolid added to an SSRI) is the highest-specificity bedside picture for serotonin syndrome by the Hunter Criteria.

sources.label: RANZCP CPG NMS vs Serotonin Syndrome

stem:
You are the registrar at a refugee health clinic seeing a 31-year-old asymptomatic man who arrived from a high TB prevalence country eight months ago. He has had a positive QuantiFERON-TB Gold Plus assay as part of his refugee health screening. He denies cough, fever, night sweats, weight loss or haemoptysis. He has no past history of TB and no known contact with active TB. He shares a household with his wife and two children, all of whom are well.

On examination he is well, with normal observations. Chest examination is clear and he has no lymphadenopathy. Chest radiograph today is reported as showing no parenchymal infiltrate, no upper-zone scarring, no cavitation, no hilar lymphadenopathy and no pleural effusion. HIV serology is negative, hepatitis B and C serology negative, full blood count and electrolytes within normal limits and his ALT is 28 U/L (<40). His eGFR is above 60 mL/min. He is not pregnant, is a non-drinker and is medically well.

data_table.Vital signs: Pulse 72/min, BP 122/76 mmHg, RR 14/min, SpO2 99% room air, temperature 36.7 °C
data_table.Investigations: QuantiFERON-TB Gold Plus positive; chest radiograph clear; HIV, hepatitis B and C negative; ALT 28 U/L; FBC and UEC within normal limits

lead_in: What is the most appropriate management?

options.text: Isoniazid 300 mg orally daily plus pyridoxine 25 mg daily for nine months total
options.rationale: Isoniazid monotherapy for 6 to 9 months is effective for latent TB but is no longer first-line in Australia given longer duration and higher hepatotoxicity than shorter rifamycin-containing regimens. (Source: National Tuberculosis Advisory Committee NTAC)

options.text: Rifampicin 600 mg orally daily for four months as latent TB infection treatment course
options.rationale: 4-month daily rifampicin is endorsed by the National Tuberculosis Advisory Committee as a first-line latent TB infection regimen with non-inferior efficacy to 9 months isoniazid plus better completion rates and lower hepatotoxicity. (Source: National Tuberculosis Advisory Committee NTAC; eTG Antibiotic)

options.text: Standard active TB therapy with rifampicin, isoniazid, pyrazinamide and ethambutol for six months
options.rationale: Four-drug RIPE therapy is for active TB; he is asymptomatic with a clear chest radiograph and treating him as active overtreats and exposes to greater toxicity. (Source: National Tuberculosis Advisory Committee NTAC)

options.text: Repeat QuantiFERON-TB Gold Plus in six months and only treat if conversion confirmed
options.rationale: A positive QuantiFERON in a recent immigrant from a high-prevalence country with clear chest radiograph already confirms latent TB infection; treatment is offered now to reduce reactivation risk. (Source: National Tuberculosis Advisory Committee NTAC)

options.text: No treatment given asymptomatic status with clear chest film and routine review in twelve months
options.rationale: Untreated latent TB carries a 5 to 10% lifetime reactivation risk; treatment is the standard offer for recent migrants from high-prevalence regions. (Source: National Tuberculosis Advisory Committee NTAC)

explanation.summary: Latent TB infection is diagnosed by a positive interferon-gamma release assay (QuantiFERON-TB Gold Plus) or tuberculin skin test in someone with no clinical or radiological evidence of active disease. The NTAC currently endorses several first-line regimens: 4 months daily rifampicin, 3 months weekly isoniazid plus rifapentine (3HP, supply limited in Australia), or 9 months isoniazid plus pyridoxine. Four-month rifampicin has better completion and lower hepatotoxicity than 9 months isoniazid. Treatment is prioritised in recent migrants, contacts, immunocompromised hosts and those starting biologic therapy. Baseline ALT, hepatitis serology and HIV are checked. Untreated latent TB carries 5-10% lifetime reactivation risk (much higher with HIV, biologics or transplant).
key_points: First-line LTBI: rifampicin 4 months daily, or isoniazid 9 months, or 3HP weekly
Active TB excluded by symptom screen + chest radiograph
Pyridoxine 25 mg added to isoniazid to prevent peripheral neuropathy
Lifetime reactivation risk 5-10%; higher with HIV or biologics
explanation.pearls: Four-month daily rifampicin is the NTAC first-line LTBI regimen because it has equivalent efficacy to nine-month isoniazid with about half the hepatotoxicity and significantly better completion; check for major drug interactions (warfarin, OCP, opioids) given rifampicin's CYP3A4 induction.

stem:
You are the ED registrar seeing a 24-year-old man brought in by his housemate with a one-day history of headache, neck stiffness, fever and photophobia. He has had two episodes of vomiting. He has no past medical history, no recent travel and no immunosuppression. He had a Bexsero vaccine in his teens. The housemate also says he was confused on the drive in.

On examination he has a temperature of 38.9 °C and a Glasgow coma score of 14. He has marked neck stiffness with positive Brudzinski's sign. There is no focal neurology, no papilloedema, no rash and pupils are equal and reactive. There is no recent head injury and no immunocompromise. His airway is patent. CT head is being arranged given his confusion. Blood cultures have been taken. The intern asks if she should give antibiotics now or wait for the LP.

data_table.Vital signs: Pulse 118/min, BP 122/76 mmHg, RR 22/min, SpO2 97% room air, temperature 38.9 °C
data_table.Examination: GCS 14 (E4 V4 M6), confused; neck stiffness with Brudzinski positive; pupils equal reactive; no focal deficit; no rash; no papilloedema
data_table.Investigations: Blood cultures taken; FBC, UEC pending; CT head being arranged

lead_in: What is the most appropriate immediate management?

options.text: Administer IV dexamethasone 10 mg only and wait for CT to determine antibiotic choice
options.rationale: Dexamethasone alone does not treat infection; it is adjunctive to and must accompany the first antibiotic dose. Withholding antibiotics is the dangerous part. (Source: eTG Antibiotic)

options.text: Defer empirical antibiotics until both CT head and lumbar puncture have been completed and CSF returned
options.rationale: Antibiotic delay in bacterial meningitis increases mortality by approximately 10% per hour; empiric antibiotics are given immediately and CT/LP follow without delay. (Source: eTG Antibiotic)

options.text: Give IV dexamethasone 10 mg with IV ceftriaxone 2 g now, then proceed to CT head and lumbar puncture
options.rationale: Empiric antibiotic plus adjunctive corticosteroid must be given immediately when bacterial meningitis is suspected; dexamethasone reduces mortality and neurological sequelae but only when given with or before the first antibiotic dose. Adding benzylpenicillin maintains Listeria cover in those over 50 or with risk factors, but in young adults the eTG combination is dexamethasone plus ceftriaxone; if Listeria risk exists, add benzylpenicillin (or amoxicillin). (Source: eTG Antibiotic; ANZICS Sepsis)

options.text: Administer IV aciclovir 10 mg/kg only and await CSF herpes simplex virus PCR before further escalation
options.rationale: Aciclovir is appropriate empirical cover for HSV encephalitis but is not a substitute for antibacterial cover when bacterial meningitis is the leading diagnosis; both can be given. (Source: eTG Antibiotic)

options.text: Administer single dose oral ciprofloxacin 500 mg for meningococcal post exposure prophylaxis only
options.rationale: Ciprofloxacin is a post-exposure prophylaxis option for contacts of meningococcal disease; it does not treat the index patient who needs full IV antibiotic therapy. (Source: eTG Antibiotic)

explanation.summary: Suspected acute bacterial meningitis is managed by immediate empiric IV antibiotic plus adjunctive dexamethasone, then prompt CT (if features of raised intracranial pressure or focal deficit), then lumbar puncture for diagnosis. Adult empiric therapy in Australia is IV dexamethasone 10 mg every 6 hours plus IV ceftriaxone 2 g every 12 hours, with the addition of IV benzylpenicillin 2.4 g every 4 hours (or amoxicillin) when Listeria risk is present (age >50 years, immunocompromised, pregnancy). Antibiotic delay independently predicts mortality. Dexamethasone reduces mortality in pneumococcal meningitis but must be given with or before the first antibiotic dose. Confirmed N. meningitidis requires single-dose chemoprophylaxis (ciprofloxacin 500 mg orally) for close contacts.
key_points: Adult empiric: dexamethasone 10 mg IV + ceftriaxone 2 g IV
Add benzylpenicillin/amoxicillin if Listeria risk (>50, immunocompromise, pregnancy)
Dexamethasone must be given with or before first antibiotic dose
Antibiotics before CT/LP if any delay risk
explanation.pearls: Dexamethasone benefits meningitis only when given with or before the first dose of antibiotic; once antibiotics are running, late steroid addition does not change outcome - the timing window is the rate limiter.

stem:
You are the medical registrar called to assess a 72-year-old woman on the urology ward 24 hours after an elective transurethral resection of bladder tumour. The nurse has just escalated because her observations have changed over the last hour. She has a history of type 2 diabetes on metformin and hypertension on perindopril. She has no known allergies.

On arrival she is rousable to voice, drowsy but orientated, and reports rigors. Pulse is 128/min, BP 84/52 mmHg, RR 28/min, SpO2 94% room air, temperature 38.9 °C. Capillary refill is 4 seconds and her extremities are cool. She has indwelling catheter drainage of cloudy urine. Bilateral chest is clear. Her abdomen is soft, mildly tender suprapubically without peritonism. Bedside venous blood gas shows lactate 4.8 mmol/L, pH 7.30, base excess -5. Urine culture from this morning has just been called as growing gram negative rods.

data_table.Vital signs: Pulse 128/min, BP 84/52 mmHg, RR 28/min, SpO2 94% room air, temperature 38.9 °C
data_table.Examination: Drowsy but orientated; CRT 4 sec, cool peripheries; clear chest; mild suprapubic tenderness; IDC draining cloudy urine
data_table.Investigations: VBG: lactate 4.8 mmol/L (<2), pH 7.30, BE -5; urine MCS gram negative rods on Gram stain

lead_in: What is the most appropriate immediate management bundle?

options.text: Hour-1 bundle: blood cultures, IV ceftriaxone plus gentamicin, 30 mL/kg crystalloid, ICU for vasopressors
options.rationale: This is septic shock (sepsis plus lactate >2 mmol/L despite resuscitation OR hypotension requiring vasopressors). The Surviving Sepsis hour-1 bundle and ANZICS recommendation are: measure lactate, take cultures, give broad spectrum antibiotics, give 30 mL/kg crystalloid, start vasopressors if MAP <65 despite fluids; antibiotics for urosepsis with gram-negative growth follow eTG sepsis empirical pathway. (Source: ANZICS Sepsis; eTG Antibiotic; Surviving Sepsis Campaign)

options.text: Administer IV piperacillin-tazobactam 4.5 g and arrange CT abdomen before fluids or cultures are taken
options.rationale: Tazocin is an acceptable empirical agent for complicated urosepsis but should follow cultures and accompany fluid resuscitation; imaging must not delay the hour-1 bundle. (Source: eTG Antibiotic; ANZICS Sepsis)

options.text: Give 250 mL balanced crystalloid bolus and reassess at 30 minutes before considering further fluid
options.rationale: A 250 mL bolus undertreats septic shock; the hour-1 bundle calls for 30 mL/kg of balanced crystalloid as the initial resuscitation in adults. (Source: ANZICS Sepsis)

options.text: Start IV adrenaline 0.05 mcg/kg/min via peripheral line before any crystalloid fluid resuscitation today
options.rationale: Vasopressors are escalated after fluid resuscitation has failed to restore MAP; starting vasopressors first does not address the relative hypovolaemia and is not the bundle sequence. (Source: ANZICS Sepsis)

options.text: Withhold empiric antibiotics until full microbiology sensitivities return and focus on fluid resuscitation
options.rationale: Time to antibiotics is the strongest modifiable predictor of mortality in septic shock; empiric therapy starts within the first hour. (Source: ANZICS Sepsis)

explanation.summary: Sepsis is life-threatening organ dysfunction from a dysregulated host response to infection. Septic shock is the subset with circulatory and cellular metabolic dysfunction requiring vasopressors and lactate above 2 mmol/L. The Surviving Sepsis Campaign hour-1 bundle is: measure lactate (repeat if initial >2), take blood cultures before antibiotics, administer broad spectrum antibiotics within an hour, give 30 mL/kg crystalloid (balanced solution preferred over saline) for hypotension or lactate above 4, and start vasopressors (noradrenaline first-line) if MAP remains under 65 after fluid resuscitation. Source control follows. Norepinephrine is initiated through a central line ideally but may be started peripherally if needed.
key_points: Hour-1 bundle: lactate, cultures, antibiotics, 30 mL/kg crystalloid, vasopressors if MAP <65
Noradrenaline is first-line vasopressor; adrenaline second
Empirical antibiotics within 1 hour; source control follows
Mortality rises about 7% per hour of antibiotic delay in septic shock
explanation.pearls: Each hour of delay in antibiotic administration in septic shock raises mortality by approximately 7%; the hour-1 bundle is the most evidence-supported intervention package in adult sepsis care.

subtopic_detail: Type B aortic dissection BP control
stem:
You are the medical registrar called urgently to ED for a 58-year-old man with sudden tearing chest pain radiating to his back that started 90 minutes ago. He has a history of poorly controlled hypertension and is a current smoker. ECG shows sinus tachycardia at 112/min with no acute ischaemic changes. Troponin is within normal limits. CT aortogram has just been completed and reports an uncomplicated type B aortic dissection arising distal to the left subclavian artery with no end-organ malperfusion, no rupture and no aortic diameter above 5.5 cm. He is talking in full sentences.

On arrival his BP is 192/104 mmHg in the right arm and 188/100 mmHg in the left arm. He has equal radial pulses. His chest is clear, abdomen is soft and non tender, and lower limb pulses are present and symmetric. He has had paracetamol 1 g and fentanyl 50 mcg with mild improvement in pain. The interventional radiologist and the cardiothoracic surgeon are in handover and they ask you to commence BP control. He has no asthma, no heart failure and no known allergies.

data_table.Vital signs: Pulse 112/min, BP 192/104 mmHg right arm, 188/100 mmHg left arm, RR 22/min, SpO2 98% room air, temperature 36.8 °C
data_table.Examination: Equal radial pulses; clear chest; soft non tender abdomen; symmetric lower limb pulses
data_table.Investigations: ECG sinus tachycardia 112/min no ischaemic changes; troponin within normal limits; CT aortogram: uncomplicated type B dissection distal to left subclavian, no malperfusion, no rupture, max diameter <5.5 cm

lead_in: What is the most appropriate first-line BP/HR target and agent?

options.text: Intravenous frusemide 40 mg bolus to reduce afterload via brisk diuresis and improve perfusion
options.rationale: Frusemide does not provide acute BP or rate control; it has no role in acute aortic syndrome management. (Source: NHFA/CSANZ Acute Aortic Syndromes)

options.text: Intravenous glyceryl trinitrate infusion targeting systolic BP 100-120 mmHg without rate control
options.rationale: Vasodilator without prior beta-blockade triggers reflex tachycardia which increases shear stress on the dissection flap; beta-blockade must precede or accompany vasodilatation. (Source: NHFA/CSANZ Acute Aortic Syndromes)

options.text: Oral perindopril 5 mg with planned reassessment of blood pressure and heart rate in 30 minutes
options.rationale: Oral antihypertensives do not titrate fast enough for acute dissection; IV infusion that can be adjusted minute-to-minute is required. (Source: NHFA/CSANZ Acute Aortic Syndromes)

options.text: Intravenous labetalol infusion titrated to systolic BP 100-120 mmHg and heart rate <60/min
options.rationale: Uncomplicated type B dissection management is medical anti-impulse therapy: lower shear stress (dP/dt) by first achieving HR <60/min with beta-blockade, then add vasodilator if SBP remains >120; IV labetalol's combined alpha and beta blockade achieves both in one agent and is the Australian first-line. (Source: NHFA/CSANZ Acute Aortic Syndromes; eTG Cardiovascular)

options.text: Urgent endovascular stent graft regardless of complication status or current haemodynamic stability
options.rationale: Uncomplicated type B dissection is managed medically with optimal anti-impulse therapy and surveillance; TEVAR is reserved for malperfusion, rupture, refractory pain, or rapid aortic expansion. (Source: NHFA/CSANZ Acute Aortic Syndromes)

explanation.summary: Acute aortic syndrome encompasses dissection (type A: ascending; type B: descending), intramural haematoma and penetrating aortic ulcer. Type A is a surgical emergency. Uncomplicated type B is managed medically with anti-impulse therapy targeting HR <60/min and SBP 100-120 mmHg, achieved with IV beta-blockade (labetalol or esmolol) plus vasodilator (sodium nitroprusside or GTN) if needed after HR is controlled. TEVAR is indicated for complicated type B (malperfusion, rupture, refractory pain, rapid expansion, persistent hypertension on maximum medical therapy, or aortic diameter >5.5 cm). Pain control with opioids, calm environment, transfer to monitored bed and arterial line placement complete the acute management.
key_points: Type B uncomplicated: medical anti-impulse therapy
Targets: HR <60/min, SBP 100-120 mmHg
Labetalol IV combines alpha and beta blockade (Australian first-line)
TEVAR for malperfusion, rupture, refractory pain, expansion
explanation.pearls: Beta-blockade must precede any vasodilator in aortic dissection - reflex tachycardia from unopposed vasodilatation raises wall shear (dP/dt) and can extend the dissection.

stem:
You are the surgical registrar called to the ED for a 62-year-old man with type 2 diabetes on metformin and gliclazide who has presented with 36 hours of progressive right lower limb redness, swelling and pain. He recalls a minor superficial scratch from gardening four days ago. He has had a fever of 38.5 °C at home and felt increasingly unwell over the last 12 hours.

On examination he is unwell, with a pulse of 116/min, BP 102/68 mmHg, RR 22/min, SpO2 96% on room air, temperature 38.7 °C. Erythema extends from the ankle to the mid-calf with poorly defined borders. The skin is tense and there is severe pain on light palpation that he reports as out of proportion to the visible signs. There are no obvious skin blisters, no crepitus and no necrotic patches. Bloods this morning: WCC 22.4 x10^9/L (4-11), CRP 280 mg/L (<5), sodium 131 mmol/L (135-145), creatinine 142 micromol/L (60-110), glucose 14.8 mmol/L, haemoglobin 138 g/L. He has no warfarin and no immunosuppression.

data_table.Vital signs: Pulse 116/min, BP 102/68 mmHg, RR 22/min, SpO2 96% room air, temperature 38.7 °C
data_table.Examination: Right lower limb erythema ankle to mid-calf with poorly defined borders; tense skin; pain out of proportion to visible signs; no blisters, crepitus or necrosis
data_table.Investigations: WCC 22.4 x10^9/L; CRP 280 mg/L; sodium 131 mmol/L; creatinine 142 micromol/L; glucose 14.8 mmol/L; Hb 138 g/L

lead_in: What is the most appropriate next step?

options.text: Order an urgent MRI of the right lower limb before any antibiotic escalation or surgical referral decision
options.rationale: MRI can show fascial enhancement and gas but is not specific and must not delay urgent surgical exploration when the clinical picture is concerning. (Source: ANZSA Necrotising Soft Tissue Infection)

options.text: Intravenous flucloxacillin 2 g six hourly and reassess clinical progress and inflammatory markers in 24 hours
options.rationale: Flucloxacillin alone is appropriate for uncomplicated cellulitis but inadequate for necrotising soft tissue infection which is polymicrobial or group A streptococcal; the syndrome requires both surgery and broad spectrum cover. (Source: eTG Antibiotic)

options.text: Outline the erythema in marker pen, elevate the limb and repeat inflammatory blood markers in 12 hours
options.rationale: Conservative observation is appropriate for stable uncomplicated cellulitis; with systemic toxicity, hyponatraemia and disproportionate pain, delay is dangerous. (Source: ANZSA Necrotising Soft Tissue Infection)

options.text: Urgent surgical exploration with on-call team plus IV piperacillin-tazobactam, clindamycin and vancomycin
options.rationale: Pain out of proportion to visible signs, systemic toxicity, hyponatraemia and high LRINEC score (>=6 here) are the bedside red flags for necrotising fasciitis; the LRINEC score is supportive but should not delay surgical exploration which is both diagnostic and therapeutic. (Source: ANZSA Necrotising Soft Tissue Infection; eTG Antibiotic; LRINEC score)

options.text: Discharge home on oral cephalexin 500 mg six hourly with general practitioner review in 48 hours
options.rationale: Discharge home with oral antibiotics in a systemically unwell diabetic with red flags is unsafe; admission is mandatory. (Source: eTG Antibiotic)

explanation.summary: Necrotising fasciitis is a surgical emergency with a mortality of 20-40%. The LRINEC score uses CRP, WCC, haemoglobin, sodium, creatinine and glucose; scores >=6 suggest probable necrotising fasciitis, >=8 makes it highly likely. The score is supportive only - clinical features (pain out of proportion, systemic toxicity, rapid progression, skin signs evolving over hours, gas, crepitus, necrosis) drive decisions and should not be overridden by a low score. Empirical antibiotics are broad: piperacillin-tazobactam (or meropenem) plus clindamycin (anti-toxin effect at the ribosome reducing streptococcal exotoxin production) plus vancomycin for MRSA cover. IVIG is considered for streptococcal toxic shock. Surgical debridement is mandatory, with relooks every 24 hours until source-controlled.
key_points: Pain out of proportion + systemic toxicity = NSTI until proven otherwise
LRINEC >=6 probable, >=8 highly likely (supportive only)
Empirical: piperacillin-tazobactam + clindamycin + vancomycin
Surgical debridement must not be delayed for imaging
explanation.pearls: Hyponatraemia (sodium <135) plus tachycardia plus pain out of proportion in a limb cellulitis is the classic triad that should trigger surgical referral for necrotising fasciitis exclusion regardless of LRINEC score.

stem:
You are the GP registrar at a sexual health clinic seeing a 28-year-old man who reports a flu-like illness with fever, sore throat, generalised lymphadenopathy and a transient truncal maculopapular rash that began 14 days ago and lasted 7 days. He has now fully recovered. He is a man who has sex with men and reports a single condomless receptive anal intercourse encounter with a new partner 35 days ago. He did not start HIV pre-exposure prophylaxis. He last tested HIV-negative 6 months ago.

On examination he is well, with normal observations and no rash today. Mild residual cervical lymphadenopathy is present. He has had no recent travel and no other risk exposures. His past medical history is unremarkable and he takes no medications. He has had a recent gonorrhoea/chlamydia panel (negative one month ago) and the practice has 4th generation HIV antigen/antibody immunoassay (HIV Ag/Ab combo) available, as well as HIV viral load and rapid antibody testing.

data_table.Vital signs: Pulse 72/min, BP 118/72 mmHg, RR 14/min, SpO2 99% room air, temperature 36.8 °C
data_table.Examination: Well; mild residual cervical lymphadenopathy; no rash today
data_table.History: MSM, condomless receptive anal intercourse 35 days ago; flu-like illness 14 days ago lasting 7 days; HIV negative 6 months prior; no PrEP

lead_in: What is the most appropriate diagnostic test today?

options.text: HIV-1 RNA viral load assay alone as the sole diagnostic test without any immunoassay today
options.rationale: Viral load testing detects HIV earlier than immunoassays (window approximately 10 days) and is reserved for suspected acute infection when 4th-gen is negative or for confirming acute primary HIV in selected cases. (Source: ASHM Acute HIV)

options.text: Fourth-generation HIV antigen/antibody combination immunoassay (HIV Ag/Ab combo)
options.rationale: At 35 days post-exposure with prior clinical seroconversion features, the 4th generation HIV Ag/Ab assay has a window period of approximately 18-45 days and reliably detects p24 antigen plus IgM/IgG antibodies; ASHM recommends 4th-gen as first-line and a reactive result triggers confirmatory testing. (Source: ASHM HIV Testing Policy; ASHM Acute HIV)

options.text: Rapid point-of-care HIV antibody test only (third generation rapid antibody assay)
options.rationale: Third generation rapid antibody tests have a longer window period (approximately 23-90 days) and miss acute primary HIV with high antigen load but low antibody titre; 4th generation is preferred. (Source: ASHM HIV Testing Policy)

options.text: Repeat HIV testing in three months without performing any laboratory tests on her samples today
options.rationale: Three-month deferral misses the diagnostic window where antiretroviral initiation reduces transmission and disease progression; testing now identifies acute infection. (Source: ASHM HIV Testing Policy)

options.text: Lymph node biopsy of the cervical lymphadenopathy to characterise the underlying pathology
options.rationale: Reactive lymphadenopathy in this context does not require biopsy; serological and virological HIV diagnosis is non-invasive and definitive. (Source: ASHM HIV Testing Policy)

explanation.summary: Fourth generation HIV assays detect both p24 antigen and HIV-1/2 IgM/IgG antibodies, with a window period of approximately 18-45 days from infection. This is the Australian first-line HIV test per ASHM. HIV-1 RNA viral load can detect infection earlier (approximately 10 days post-exposure) and is reserved for high index of suspicion when 4th-gen is negative, suspected acute primary HIV, or seroconversion illness. Confirmatory testing of any reactive screening assay uses HIV-1/2 antibody differentiation and Western blot. Early diagnosis enables prompt antiretroviral initiation which reduces transmission (treatment-as-prevention) and disease progression. Notification to the public health unit is mandatory.
key_points: 4th gen HIV Ag/Ab combo: window approximately 18-45 days, first-line
HIV RNA viral load: window approximately 10 days, for suspected acute primary HIV
Rapid 3rd-gen tests miss acute infection
Notify to public health unit on confirmed diagnosis
explanation.pearls: Suspected HIV seroconversion illness presenting at day 35 post-exposure: 4th generation Ag/Ab combo is first line and HIV-1 RNA is added when the immunoassay is negative or equivocal but suspicion remains high.

stem:
You are the inpatient medical registrar reviewing a 54-year-old woman admitted 72 hours ago with community-acquired pneumonia. She presented with five days of cough, fever and pleuritic chest pain. She was assessed in ED as SMART-COP score 3 (BP 102/68, RR 28, SpO2 91% room air, multilobar infiltrate), commenced on IV benzylpenicillin 1.2 g 6-hourly plus oral doxycycline 100 mg twice daily and admitted to the ward.

On day 3 of treatment she is no better. Her temperature has continued spiking to 38.8 °C twice daily. Her oxygen requirement has risen from 2 to 4 L/min nasal prongs. She has a new mild confusion that her family confirms is out of character. She has developed loose stools twice in the last 12 hours but no abdominal pain. Sodium has fallen from 138 to 128 mmol/L, ALT is now 220 U/L (was 32 U/L) and CRP has risen from 184 to 256 mg/L. Repeat chest radiograph shows progression of the right lower zone consolidation with a small new left mid zone infiltrate. Sputum cultures are negative. She returned a week ago from a hot springs holiday in the Northern Territory. Urinary Legionella antigen has not yet been sent.

data_table.Vital signs: Pulse 108/min, BP 108/68 mmHg, RR 26/min, SpO2 91% on 4 L nasal prongs, temperature 38.8 °C
data_table.Examination: Mild confusion (new); right lower zone crackles plus new left mid zone crackles; abdomen soft
data_table.Investigations: Sodium 128 mmol/L (was 138, ref 135-145); ALT 220 U/L (was 32, ref <40); CRP 256 mg/L (was 184); chest radiograph: progression right lower + new left mid zone; sputum culture negative

lead_in: What is the most appropriate next step?

options.text: Bronchoscopy with BAL today to obtain definitive microbiological diagnosis before changing antibiotics
options.rationale: Bronchoscopy is useful in non resolving pneumonia but should not delay empiric escalation when clinical and biochemical clues already strongly suggest Legionella; urinary antigen and BAL can be sent in parallel. (Source: eTG Antibiotic)

options.text: Escalate to IV piperacillin-tazobactam plus vancomycin for nosocomial pneumonia
options.rationale: She has community acquired pneumonia (presented from home) and there is no evidence of pseudomonal or MRSA risk; broad spectrum nosocomial cover does not address suspected atypical Legionella infection. (Source: eTG Antibiotic)

options.text: Continue current therapy and add IV hydrocortisone 50 mg 6-hourly for severe CAP
options.rationale: Corticosteroids may have benefit in severe CAP but do not substitute for adequate antimicrobial cover when an atypical pathogen is being missed by the current regimen. (Source: eTG Antibiotic)

options.text: Send urinary Legionella antigen and switch to IV azithromycin 500 mg daily plus continue beta-lactam cover
options.rationale: The triad of hyponatraemia, transaminitis and gastrointestinal symptoms in a patient with progressive multifocal pneumonia and recent exposure to a warm-water source is highly suggestive of Legionella pneumophila, which requires azithromycin (or a fluoroquinolone such as moxifloxacin) for adequate coverage; doxycycline has weaker activity against severe Legionella infection and the urinary antigen test should be sent immediately to confirm. (Source: eTG Antibiotic: Community-acquired pneumonia; CDNA Legionellosis SoNG)

options.text: Transfer to ICU for non invasive ventilation without changing antibiotic regimen
options.rationale: Oxygen requirements may indeed warrant higher level care, but escalation of respiratory support without addressing the inadequately covered pathogen perpetuates clinical failure. (Source: ANZICS Sepsis)

explanation.summary: Treatment failure in community acquired pneumonia at 72 hours is defined as worsening clinical status or absence of improvement and prompts re-evaluation. Causes include inadequate antimicrobial cover (atypical pathogens such as Legionella, Mycoplasma, Chlamydophila), resistant organism, complication (empyema, abscess), wrong diagnosis (PE, vasculitis, eosinophilic pneumonia, malignancy) or host factor (immunosuppression). Legionnaires disease classically presents with hyponatraemia, transaminitis, diarrhoea, relative bradycardia and progressive multilobar consolidation; the urinary antigen is sensitive for serogroup 1 (most common). First-line therapy in Australia is azithromycin or a respiratory fluoroquinolone (moxifloxacin); doxycycline has less activity against severe Legionella. Source identification (cooling towers, spa pools, warm water systems) triggers public health notification.
key_points: CAP non-response at 72 h: rethink pathogen, complication, diagnosis, host
Legionella triad: hyponatraemia + transaminitis + diarrhoea
Urinary antigen sensitive for serogroup 1
First-line: azithromycin IV or moxifloxacin; doxycycline weaker
explanation.pearls: The classic Legionella signature in an otherwise typical-looking CAP is the combination of hyponatraemia under 130 mmol/L, ALT above 100 U/L and gastrointestinal symptoms; presence of a warm-water exposure (cooling tower, spa, hot tub) clinches the suspicion even before urinary antigen returns.
